notable.notable_blurb: Wallingford pastor 44 years; Yale trustee from 1732

narrative_blocks.header: One of five in Yale's class of 1705
narrative_blocks.detail: An 1878 historical marker in Wallingford calls him 'one of the most eminent preachers and faithful ministers in the colony, known for the vigor and penetration of his mind.' He was twice invited (1710, 1711) to go to Nova Scotia regarding the capture of Port Royal — and twice declined. In 1712 he was appointed to oversee the benefit of Connecticut's copper mine in what is now Cheshire. His son Samuel (I00020) graduated Yale in 1729; his son Chauncey (I00074) graduated in 1738 — a second-generation Yale family. His daughter Katherine married James Dana, later a prominent New Haven minister. (HMDB; Whittlesey Genealogy)
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Whittlesey graduated in Yale's five-member class of 1705, during only the second year of the college's existence in Saybrook. By 1710 he was installed as pastor at Wallingford — a position he held until his death in 1752. From 1732 he also served as a Yale trustee.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale trustee for his last twenty years
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained at Wallingford in 1710, he held that pulpit forty-four years and joined Yale’s board in 1732, serving until his death. Two of his sons married toward this tree — among them Chauncey, the New Haven preacher.

sources.notes: Monument inscription preserved; Bacon's Historical Collections I, 239

notable.notable_blurb: Milford pastor thirty years, Yale and Harvard honors

narrative_blocks.header: His father preached his ordination sermon
narrative_blocks.body: When Samuel was ordained at Milford on December 9, 1737, the sermon was preached by his own father, Rev. Samuel Whittelsey of Wallingford — and then published, the elder man’s fourth and final printed work.

narrative_blocks.header: A Latin stone, translated for the curious
narrative_blocks.body: His Milford monument, in Latin, credits him with honors from both Yale and Harvard and “virtues, piety and good deeds” that “everywhere shone with peculiar lustre” across more than thirty years in the town’s pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Milford minister, then Middletown statesman
narrative_blocks.body: Her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey served Milford for thirty-plus years until his death in 1768. She remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — described as 'distinguished in civil and military life.'

narrative_blocks.header: Whittlesey genealogy: 'lady of high intellectual qualities'
narrative_blocks.body: The Whittlesey genealogy describes her as 'a lady of high intellectual and moral qualities.' This was an unusual tribute for a colonial woman, whose lives were typically invisible in family records of the era.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived both husbands; died at 87 in Middletown
narrative_blocks.body: She outlived her first husband Rev. Samuel Whittlesey by 35 years and her second husband Jabez Hamlin as well. She died at Middletown in 1803, age 87.

parents.mother_research_notes: Susannah Newton Whittlesey Hamlin (H00142, b. Jan 14, 1716, d. May 10, 1803). Per Whittlesey Genealogy #42 child line: Samuel Whittlesey #42 (I00020) m. Susannah Newton Sept 21, 1743; their fourth child was #135 Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097). After Samuel's death in 1768, Susannah remarried Hon. Jabez Hamlin of Middletown — hence her 'Hamlin' display name. STAGE 6Y-11 REVERT NOTE: In Stage 6Y-10 I incorrectly 'fixed' this from H00142 to H00143 (Martha Newton Whittlesey). That was backwards — the two Newton sisters married two Whittlesey brothers: Susannah → Samuel #42, Martha → Chauncey #88. Roger Newton's mother is Susannah, NOT Martha. Reverted in 6Y-11 per book #42 confirmation.

enlistment_record.text: Signed "We, the Subscribers, being convinced of the necessity of a body of forces to defeat certain Wicked Purposes formed by the instruments on Ministerial Tyranny do solemnly engage ourselves and enlist as private soldiers..." Dated at Litchfield. Signed: ROGER N. WHITTELSEY.

open_questions.question: Is Elisha Whittlesey (b. Mar 8, 1783, Southbury CT; d. Jan 7, 1863) a missing child of Roger Newton Whittlesey?
open_questions.notes: Elisha Whittlesey (1783-1863) was born in Southbury CT, studied law under Tapping Reeve at Litchfield CT, moved to Canfield, Mahoning County OH in 1806, served as U.S. Congressman from Ohio and comptroller of the U.S. Treasury. His birth year (1783) falls exactly between documented children HD0105 (Chauncey, b.1781) and HD0106 (Susan, b.1784), suggesting he may be a missing child of this entry. Roger Newton Whittlesey (HD0097) lived in Litchfield County CT — where Tapping Reeve's famous law school was located. If confirmed, Elisha is a gen 6 Hooker descendant, and his 20-year law partnership with Eben Newton (HD2927) — also a gen 6 Hooker descendant — becomes a documented civic_peer cross_connection. Action: Check Whittlesey genealogy for Southbury CT branch; Litchfield County records 1783.
open_questions.resolution: Elisha Whittlesey (b. Oct 19, 1783 Washington CT) is Whittlesey book entry #310, son of John Whittlesey (#123, b.1741 Newington CT). His line descends from John Whittlesey of Saybrook through Eliphalet→Eliphalet→John→Elisha — a completely different branch from the project's Whittlesey line (which runs through Rev. Samuel Whittlesey of Wallingford/Milford). Elisha is NOT a Hooker descendant.

military_service.unit: Col. Andrew Ward Jr.'s Regiment, Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company
military_service.notes: Enlisted Jan 21, 1776 at Litchfield for expedition to New York under Gen. Charles Lee. Eight-week term. His musket was appraised as part of the company arms Jan 26, 1776.

narrative_blocks.header: Eight-week militia tour to defend New York 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Roger enlisted at twenty-one in Capt. Bezaleel Beebe's Company under Col. Andrew Ward Jr. and Maj. Gen. Charles Lee in January 1776 — orders reaching Capt. Beebe on a Sunday, the company armed and on the march toward Fairfield by Saturday. The eight-week tour took him to defend New York City, his only documented Revolutionary service.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the peace, Litchfield County, many years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war Roger settled at South Farms in Litchfield County and served as justice of the peace for many years, the position of standing in the rural Connecticut hierarchy. He worked his farm there until his death at eighty-one in March 1835.

narrative_blocks.header: Father of thirteen children with Ann Woodruff
narrative_blocks.body: Roger married Ann Woodruff of Litchfield in April 1775, the same month as Lexington, and they raised thirteen children together. The youngest, George Washington Whittlesey, was born in 1799 when Roger was forty-five; Ann lived until 1825.

education.notes: 1764 A.M. at Yale. Whittlesey genealogy: "132. Samuel graduated at Yale; 1764, A.M. at Yale."

narrative_blocks.header: Righteous heaven took him at thirty
narrative_blocks.body: The young Milford physician — “endowed with good natural abilities and furnished by education for public service” — died in his thirty-first year, leaving no children. His widow Mary remarried a Yale tutor; her own gravestone, raised by that second husband, reads “To part was agony.”

cross_connections.display_label: Ezra Stiles held his hand at death — received a final squeeze
Eulogized at his 1787 deathbed by Yale's Stiles
cross_connections.notes: President Ezra Stiles (HD0346) attended Whittlesey's 1787 deathbed; per the diary record, Stiles took his hand and asked 'Do you feel now the assurance of hope?' and Chauncey rallied his dying strength to answer 'Yes.' Stiles published a eulogy describing him as 'oracular at college' for breadth of learning. Per Whittlesey Genealogy #88.

narrative_blocks.header: 'No more grace than a chair' — Brainerd's verdict
narrative_blocks.detail: Brainerd later repented of the comment. President Ezra Stiles had a different assessment of Whittlesey: 'In literature he was in his day oracular at college, for he taught with facility and success in every branch of knowledge.' Whittlesey had graduated Yale in 1738, continued as a resident graduate on Bishop Berkeley's foundation, and served as a tutor from 1739 to 1745 — the very years of the Awakening's height. His diary entry from this period reflects the opposite of Brainerd's caricature: 'Having repeatedly commended my case to God by prayer... I am as yet too little acquainted with God, the Scriptures, human nature in general, and my own heart in particular to venture to undertake the great and important work of the ministry.' (Whittlesey Genealogy)
narrative_blocks.body: During the Great Awakening of the 1740s, Yale student David Brainerd declared that Chauncey Whittlesey had 'no more grace than a chair.' Yale expelled Brainerd for it. The Whittlesey genealogy records the remark as evidence of the 'rash, pharisaical, and censorious spirit' of the New Light evangelicals — not of Whittelsey's actual faith.

narrative_blocks.header: Ezra Stiles at Whittlesey's deathbed, July 1787
narrative_blocks.detail: Dr. Dana wrote of Whittlesey's death: 'His old age was amiably splendid as a clear setting sun. His past days looked back upon him with a smile of friendship, and the morning of immortal felicity dawning on his soul gloriously irradiated the valley of death.' Whittlesey had served as minister of First Church New Haven for nearly thirty years (1758–1787), after a decade as a New Haven merchant and colonial assemblyman. The handshake with Stiles — one Yale man to another — was his last act of communication. (Whittlesey Genealogy; EH 1909)
narrative_blocks.body: As Whittlesey lay dying in July 1787, his close friend Ezra Stiles — Yale president and Hooker descendant — came to the room. Taking his hand, Stiles asked: 'Do you feel now the assurance of hope? If you would say yes, and cannot speak, answer me by the pressure of your hand.' Whittlesey rallied his dying strength and pressed back.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached to the wartime Assembly
narrative_blocks.body: On May 14, 1778, with the Revolution undecided, Whittelsey delivered the Election Sermon before Connecticut’s General Assembly on II Samuel 23:3-4 — “the importance of religion in the civil Ruler.” The Assembly ordered it printed and sent Samuel Bishop and Eneas Munson to deliver its formal thanks.

bio.bio_blurb: New Haven pastor in the Awakening’s crossfire

sources.page: Entry 88 (under Chauncey Whittlesey, his first wife)

narrative_blocks.header: Twelve documented children with Chauncey Whittlesey
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton Whittlesey and her husband Chauncey Whittlesey had twelve documented children between 1754 and 1773 — a high birth count even for the colonial-era marriages of leading New England families. Of the twelve, five died before reaching adulthood: Elizabeth (born 1758, died 1758), Elizabeth (born 1760, died 1760), John Bryan (born 1761, died 1763), plus Newton (died 1785 at thirty-one) and Susannah (died 1797 at thirty-one).

narrative_blocks.header: Two consecutive daughters named Elizabeth died young
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey lost two consecutive daughters both named Elizabeth — one born 1758 died the same year, one born 1760 died the same year. The replacement-naming pattern (same first name given to a later child after an earlier one died young) was characteristic of colonial New England families seeking to preserve a chosen name through high infant mortality. A third Elizabeth, born 1773, finally survived to age ninety-three and never married.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Susannah married Judge Dyer White
narrative_blocks.body: Martha and Chauncey's daughter Susannah Whittlesey (1766-1797) married Judge Dyer White — connecting the Whittlesey line into the Connecticut bench. The Whittlesey family across this generation produced lawyers, judges, military officers, and merchants spreading across New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Milford 1729, lived to age eighty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Newton was born January 27, 1729 in Milford Connecticut, daughter of Colonel Roger Newton, and died October 27, 1812 at age eighty-three. She married Chauncey Whittlesey on August 13, 1753 as his second wife — his first wife Elizabeth Whiting had died October 17, 1751. She survived her husband by many years.

bio.bio_blurb: Connecticut woman who lived to ninety-two

career.notes: Established merchant practice in Middletown Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister and brother both married Van Deursens
narrative_blocks.body: Martha married Capt. William Van Deursen of Middletown in September 1778; her younger brother Samuel married William's sister Sarah Van Deursen at the Reformed Dutch Church in New York City that December. Two Whittlesey siblings married two Van Deursen siblings within three months — a tight kinship knot.

career.notes: Practiced at New Haven; appointed Justice of the Peace.
Removed with son Isaac Newton; died there March 7, 1838.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1779; attorney in Connecticut, New Jersey, New York, and Indiana.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Van Deursen sister to brother's wife
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel married Sarah Van Deursen at the Reformed Dutch Church in New York City on December 10, 1778. Sarah was the sister of Capt. William Van Deursen, the Middletown sea captain who had married Samuel's elder sister Martha two months earlier — a double knot tying Whittleseys to Van Deursens.

career.organization: 3rd company, 1st regiment under Gen. David Wooster (siege of Boston)
career.notes: Per Whittlesey Genealogy #155 + Connecticut Men in the Revolution.
Marched from Boston to New York with Gen. Washington; engaged at White Plains, Trenton, Princeton.

military_service.unit: 3rd company, 1st regiment under Gen. David Wooster (1775); Capt. Hale's company, 19th Continental Regiment (1776)
military_service.notes: Per Whittlesey Genealogy #155 + Connecticut Men in the Revolution. AGE ANOMALY: book records enlistment as sergeant May 15, 1775 — would make Charles age 10 (born Oct 18, 1764). Promotion to 2nd lieutenant March 1776 at age 11 also unusual. Possible book error or conflation with another Charles Whittlesey of similar name.

narrative_blocks.header: Marched with Washington from Boston to Princeton
narrative_blocks.body: Per the Whittlesey Genealogy, Charles served as sergeant under Gen. Wooster May to December 1775 (siege of Boston), then as 2nd lieutenant in Capt. Hale's 19th Continental Regiment from March 1776, marching with Washington to the victories at White Plains, Trenton, and Princeton. Whether the dates are accurate or conflate another Charles, the book records his Continental service explicitly.

narrative_blocks.header: She and Judge White died one year apart
narrative_blocks.body: Susannah married Judge Dyer White on March 18, 1791. He died October 2, 1796; exactly one year later, October 2, 1797, Susannah herself died — leaving the calendar marking their parallel passings.

career.notes: Resided New Haven greater part of life.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1791; later US inspector at New York.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard's second president
narrative_blocks.detail: Chauncy was educated at Trinity College, Cambridge, and served as a Church of England minister before emigrating under Puritan pressure. At Plymouth he became minister of Scituate, then succeeded Henry Dunster as Harvard's president in 1654. He held the post for 18 years. His son Nathaniel (X01902) graduated Harvard in 1661 and became minister of Hatfield, Massachusetts. His grandson Nathaniel Chauncey (X01904) received Yale's first M.A. degree in 1702 and served as Durham's minister for 50 years. His great-granddaughter Sarah Chauncey married Rev. Samuel Whittlesey (X01900) of Wallingford, whose son Chauncey Whittlesey (I00074) married Martha Newton — a 4th-generation descendant of Thomas Hooker. (Wikipedia)
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Chauncy served as the second President of Harvard College from 1654 until his death in 1672. A Cambridge-educated Puritan who emigrated to Plymouth Colony in 1638, he was one of the most rigorous classical scholars in early New England — and the patriarch of a remarkable dynasty of Yale and Harvard clergy.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard kept him until seventy-nine
narrative_blocks.body: The Trinity College, Cambridge scholar emigrated to Plymouth Colony in 1638 and took Harvard’s presidency in 1654 — holding it eighteen years until his death in office in 1672, at seventy-nine.
